evidence: `crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs:7-15` imports server-level domain types (`ServerEventStore`, `SpecRegistry`, verification result/status) plus backend adapters (`temper_store_postgres`, `temper_store_turso`), making CLI startup code a cross-layer dependency hub.

`crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs:212-369` duplicates almost identical registry restoration workflows for Postgres and Turso, so boundary behavior is copy-maintained rather than owned behind one backend-neutral seam.

`crates/temper-server/src/events.rs:46-51`, `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs:22-30`, and `crates/temper-server/src/state/dispatch_blocking.rs:24-49` all reach into broad `ServerState` capabilities from unrelated concerns, reinforcing service-locator coupling across modules.
`crates/temper-cli/src/init/mod.rs:56-64` scaffolds local `evolution/{observations,problems,analyses,decisions,insights}` directories as a first-class project structure.

`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs:27-33` and `:95-98` declare Turso/persistent store as the runtime source for evolution lists/insights, and `:77-85`/`:131-135` return empty payloads when no persistent store exists, with no file-based fallback.
The project shape advertised by `temper init` and the runtime ownership of evolution data diverge, so top-level ownership is split between scaffolded filesystem concepts and DB-governed behavior.

IOA action/guard/effect translation logic is duplicated across runtime and verifier builders, including the same name-heuristic fallback for add/remove item actions (crates/temper-jit/src/table/builder.rs:35-177, crates/temper-verify/src/model/builder.rs:80-188).

Several public convenience APIs hide fallibility by panicking (`from_ioa_source`, `register_tenant`, bootstrap helpers), which makes interface contracts dishonest about failure modes (crates/temper-jit/src/table/builder.rs:15-18, crates/temper-server/src/registry.rs:236-252, crates/temper-platform/src/bootstrap.rs:87-107).

TursoEventStore has become a broad catch-all abstraction (1943 LOC, 34 public async methods) spanning specs, trajectories, evolution records, decisions, constraints, wasm, and policies, indicating low-cohesion boundary design (crates/temper-store-turso/src/store.rs:1-220, crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs:223-301).

Evolution operations drop caller identity at integration seams by dispatching system entities with `AgentContext::default()`, while post-dispatch effects explicitly preserve agent/session context for attribution (crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/operations.rs:48-55,262-270, crates/temper-server/src/state/dispatch/effects.rs:119-148).

Tenant resolution seam is implicit and order-dependent: missing tenant headers silently fall back to first registered tenant or `TenantId::default()`, which couples request routing to registry state rather than explicit boundary input (crates/temper-server/src/odata/common.rs:13-37, crates/temper-platform/src/bootstrap.rs:142-151).

CLI `serve::run` orchestrates storage selection, registry recovery, policy hydration, wasm recovery, bootstrap, router/listener wiring, and background workers in one seam-heavy function, increasing handoff complexity and making boundary regressions harder to isolate (crates/temper-cli/src/serve/mod.rs:60-340).

`crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs` keeps a `legacy` shim with `#[allow(deprecated)] pub use crate::store::RecordStore;`, but the shim itself is not marked deprecated, so old usage remains silent and open-ended.

Multiple assigned modules are pass-through veneers with no local behavior (`crates/temper-mcp/src/repl.rs`, `crates/temper-platform/src/agent/mod.rs`, `crates/temper-platform/src/deploy/mod.rs`, `crates/temper-odata/src/query/mod.rs`), adding indirection without local policy.

Error surfaces across assigned crates still rely heavily on string payloads (`AuthzError::* (String)`, `McpError::* (String)`), which encourages ad-hoc message parsing and weakens precise handling at call sites.

`holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles["crates/temper-server/src/"]` reports 22 files in one flat root folder, mixing routing (`router.rs`), auth helpers (`authz_helpers.rs`), constraints (`constraint_engine.rs`), dispatch facade (`dispatch.rs`), and other domains.
`crates/temper-server/src/dispatch.rs` is a compatibility facade that re-exports both OData handlers and request-context types, which blurs placement/ownership boundaries.

`crates/temper-server/src/observe/entities.rs` imports tenant extraction through `crate::dispatch::extract_tenant`, tying observe package placement to an OData-oriented facade instead of a direct request-context boundary.

`crates/temper-cli/src/serve/mod.rs:60` `run` combines storage bootstrapping, registry recovery, policy hydration, wasm recovery, tenant bootstraps, router/listener startup, UI process spawning, and verification scheduling in one entrypoint.

`crates/temper-server/src/odata/read.rs:281` `handle_odata_get_for_tenant` and `crates/temper-server/src/odata/write.rs:147`/`:291`/`:403`/`:494` each inline path parsing, entity-set/type resolution, existence checks, invariant checks, and response shaping instead of sharing workflow helpers.

`crates/temper-server/src/state/dispatch/wasm.rs:24` `dispatch_wasm_integrations_internal` handles integration lookup, authz setup, secret templating, invocation, telemetry, callback dispatch, and governance/pending-decision side effects; `record_wasm_authz_denial` (`:420`) adds additional persistence/entity-creation concerns.

`crates/temper-store-turso/src/store.rs` (1943 LOC) centralizes migrations plus unrelated CRUD/query responsibilities (specs, trajectories, feature requests, evolution records, design-time events, pending decisions, tenant policies, wasm modules/logs), making one module the default sink for new persistence concerns.

`crates/temper-server/src/api/decisions.rs:62` and `crates/temper-server/src/api/policies.rs:33` embed authorization, validation, policy compilation/reload, persistence, and audit/evolution side effects directly in handlers, producing duplicated orchestration patterns across APIs.

`SecurityContext::from_headers` accepts `x-temper-principal-kind: system` from request headers (`crates/temper-authz/src/context.rs:65-74`), and `AuthzEngine::authorize_or_bypass` unconditionally allows system principals (`crates/temper-authz/src/engine.rs:217-229`).

`extract_tenant` silently falls back to first registered tenant or `TenantId::default()` when `x-tenant-id` is absent (`crates/temper-server/src/odata/common.rs:13-37`), creating implicit tenant selection in a multi-tenant server.

Observe authorization is action-only with empty resource attrs (`crates/temper-server/src/authz_helpers.rs:60-65`), while handlers return cross-tenant data directly (e.g., all actor keys in `crates/temper-server/src/observe/entities.rs:30-51`, all tenant specs in `crates/temper-server/src/observe/specs.rs:30-67`).

Authz telemetry records tenant as literal `"default"` in both auth paths (`crates/temper-server/src/state/entity_ops.rs:230-237` and `277-284`), weakening denial/audit consistency for non-default tenants.

findings.summary: CLI storage module couples orchestration, backend IO, and server registry internals in one seam.
evidence: `crates/temper-cli/src/serve/storage.rs:7-15` mixes imports from server domain types and concrete storage adapters, then performs connection/migration/upsert/restore in the same module (`:39-127`, `:212-369`, `:472-499`).

`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/records_list.rs:28-31` depends on runtime persistent-store behavior that this CLI module effectively shapes at startup, creating a wide blast radius for persistence changes.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a persistence bootstrap boundary (e.g., `SpecPersistenceBootstrap`) owned by the server/persistence layer; move registry restoration and backend-specific translation out of CLI, leaving CLI to call a narrow boot API.

findings.summary: Postgres and Turso registry restore flows are duplicated, causing architecture drift risk.
evidence: `load_registry_from_postgres` and `load_registry_from_turso` duplicate grouping, CSDL parsing, tenant registration, and verification-status restoration logic almost line-for-line (`storage.rs:212-294` vs `296-369`).

This duplication sits on critical startup state used by downstream runtime paths (including blocking dispatch over `ServerState` in `dispatch_blocking.rs:24-49`), so divergence in one restore path can silently alter runtime behavior per backend.
findings.suggestion: Extract a backend-agnostic restore pipeline that accepts a normalized row/constraint iterator and shared mapping callbacks; keep backend code limited to data fetch and row adaptation.

findings.summary: Scaffolded filesystem evolution model conflicts with DB-owned runtime evolution endpoints.
evidence: `init::run` always creates `evolution/*` directories (`init/mod.rs:59-63`) and tests lock this shape in (`:158-162`).

`list_evolution_records` and `list_evolution_insights` only read through `state.persistent_store()` (`records_list.rs:28-31`, `:96-98`) and otherwise return empty lists (`:77-85`, `:131-135`) without consuming scaffolded folders.
findings.suggestion: Choose one canonical ownership model for evolution records: either remove the scaffolded `evolution/*` directories from `temper init` or add a first-class file-backed evolution store path that these handlers can query.

findings.summary: Broad `ServerState` access pattern creates implicit cross-module dependencies.
evidence: SSE handler directly consumes auth + event bus from full state (`events.rs:49-51`), evolution handlers consume auth + persistence from full state (`records_list.rs:26-30`, `:94-97`), and dispatch blocking consumes internal dispatch engine via full state impl (`dispatch_blocking.rs:24-49`).
These modules depend on a single broad state carrier instead of narrow capability interfaces, so internal state changes can ripple across unrelated endpoints and dispatch paths.
findings.suggestion: Slice `ServerState` into capability traits/typed subcontexts (e.g., `EventStreamContext`, `EvolutionStoreContext`, `WasmDispatchContext`) and inject only required capabilities into each module.

findings.summary: IOA translation logic is duplicated across runtime and verifier paths, creating drift risk
evidence: Both modules independently map IOA guards/effects into internal enums with parallel match trees (jit builder: `automaton::Guard/Effect` mapping at lines 45-153; verify builder: `translate_guards/translate_effects` at lines 127-187).
Both modules replicate the same name-based fallback heuristic for add/remove item behavior when `effect` is empty (jit builder lines 156-175; verify builder lines 96-114).
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared IOA-to-intermediate translation layer in `temper-spec` (or a dedicated translation crate), and have both JIT and verify builders consume that normalized representation so semantic changes happen in one place.

findings.summary: Public convenience APIs mask fallible work behind panics instead of typed errors
evidence: `TransitionTable::from_ioa_source` panics on parse failure via `expect` (builder.rs:15-18).
`SpecRegistry::register_tenant*` wrappers panic on `try_` failures (`unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!(...))`) instead of surfacing `Result` (registry.rs:236-252,274-292).
Bootstrap flow uses `unwrap_or_else(panic!)` and `assert!` for parse/verification failures, making startup failure paths non-recoverable and untyped (bootstrap.rs:87-107).
`EntityActorHandler::with_ioa_invariants` also panics on parse via `expect` (sim_handler.rs:73-76).
findings.suggestion: Make fallible constructors and registration/bootstrap entry points return `Result` all the way up; keep panic-only wrappers private (or remove them) so interface contracts stay explicit and composable.

findings.summary: Single Turso store abstraction has grown into a low-cohesion multi-domain facade
evidence: `TursoEventStore` is 1943 LOC and exposes 34 public async methods spanning unrelated domains (specs, trajectories, evolution records, decisions, constraints, wasm, tenant policy).

Callers in evolution ops directly depend on specialized methods like `upsert_feature_request`, `list_feature_requests`, and `insert_evolution_record`, indicating domain-specific concerns are leaking through one monolithic persistence facade.
findings.suggestion: Split Turso persistence into cohesive repositories (e.g., `SpecStore`, `TrajectoryStore`, `EvolutionStore`, `PolicyStore`, `WasmStore`) behind smaller traits, then compose them in server/CLI wiring.

findings.summary: System-entity dispatch seam drops actor identity by using default agent context
evidence: Evolution operations create system entities with `dispatch_tenant_action(..., &AgentContext::default())` (operations.rs:48-51, 262-270).

Post-dispatch pipeline explicitly propagates `AgentContext` to preserve attribution for trajectories/SSE/scheduled actions (effects.rs:119-148, 228-236), so this call path is inconsistent with established seam behavior.
findings.suggestion: Thread caller `AgentContext` into evolution handlers and `create_system_entity`, and require explicit provenance (e.g., source principal) when synthetic/system contexts are intentionally used.

findings.summary: Tenant boundary is implicit and order-dependent when request tenant is missing
evidence: `extract_tenant` silently selects the first registered tenant, then `TenantId::default()` if none exists (odata/common.rs:23-37).
Bootstrap injects multiple tenants (`temper-system`, `default`, and app tenants), so fallback behavior is tied to registration state/order instead of explicit request intent (bootstrap.rs:142-151).
findings.suggestion: Require explicit tenant resolution for OData routes (header/path/query), and only allow fallback behind a clearly named compatibility mode flag with deterministic priority rules.

findings.summary: Path extraction exposes `max_paths_per_target` but never applies it
evidence: `PathExtractionConfig` defines `max_paths_per_target` (paths.rs:35-37), but `extract_paths` reconstructs and inserts a single path per target without consulting that field (paths.rs:135-150).
`VerificationCascade::with_path_extraction` advertises configurable path extraction and forwards config to `extract_paths` (cascade.rs:170-213), making the unused field a misleading API surface.
findings.suggestion: Either implement multi-path extraction honoring `max_paths_per_target` (k-shortest/alternate-parent tracking) or remove the field to keep the config contract truthful.

findings.summary: Compatibility shim keeps deprecated API path alive without compiler pressure to migrate.
evidence: `crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs` defines `pub mod legacy` and suppresses deprecation warnings via `#[allow(deprecated)]` while re-exporting `RecordStore`.
The module docs say these APIs are deprecated and will be removed, but there is no `#[deprecated(...)]` attribute on the shim entrypoint itself.
`crates/temper-evolution/src/records.rs` models migration planning (`ImplementationPlan.migration_required`) while the API surface still silently accepts legacy usage.
findings.suggestion: Mark the legacy module/re-export with `#[deprecated(note = ...)]`, remove `#[allow(deprecated)]` at the public boundary, and gate legacy exports behind an explicit compatibility feature with a sunset target.

findings.summary: Several modules are pure re-export/pass-through layers that add navigation and maintenance overhead.
evidence: `crates/temper-mcp/src/repl.rs` only re-exports `ReplConfig` and `run_repl` from `temper_sandbox::repl`.
`crates/temper-platform/src/agent/mod.rs` and `crates/temper-platform/src/deploy/mod.rs` are mostly façade re-exports without local behavior/policy.
`crates/temper-odata/src/query/mod.rs` is also a pure barrel for `filter` and `types` exports.
findings.suggestion: Collapse purely pass-through modules where possible, or keep them only when they enforce clear local policy/versioning boundaries; otherwise export directly from owning modules to reduce indirection depth.

findings.summary: Error enums rely on broad `String` payloads, reducing precision and encouraging formatting-as-contract patterns.
evidence: `AuthzError` variants (`Denied`, `PolicyParse`, `InvalidPrincipal`, `InvalidResource`, `Engine`) all carry plain `String`.
`McpError` variants are predominantly `String`-backed, even across distinct transport/protocol/runtime categories.
`ObserveError` mixes one typed source (`SerializationError(#[from] serde_json::Error)`) with multiple free-form string variants, indicating inconsistent granularity.
findings.suggestion: Introduce typed payload structs or source-error wrappers per category (e.g., dedicated parse/resource/principal error types), keep human formatting at display boundaries, and standardize conversion helpers across crates.

findings.summary: Top-level `dispatch.rs` acts as a cross-domain shim, obscuring package boundaries.
evidence: `dispatch.rs` re-exports OData handlers plus `AgentContext`/`extract_agent_context` from `request_context`, mixing two ownership domains in one facade.
`observe/entities.rs` depends on `crate::dispatch::extract_tenant`, so observe routing depends on an OData compatibility module instead of a direct request-context module.
Server root directory has 22 files with mixed concerns, indicating flattening debt around boundary files.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Create target folders/modules `crates/temper-server/src/odata/dispatch.rs` (OData handlers only) and keep tenant/agent extraction in `crates/temper-server/src/request_context.rs`. 2) Move imports in order: `router.rs` and OData call sites to `crate::odata::dispatch::*`, then observe/API call sites to `crate::request_context::*`. 3) Keep `dispatch.rs` as temporary deprecated shim for one release, then remove it and update `lib.rs` exports. Import-update/validation commands: `rg -n "crate::dispatch::" crates/temper-server/src`, `cargo check -p temper-server --features observe`, `cargo test -p temper-server --features observe`.

findings.summary: Authz and constraint modules are placed as root helpers instead of domain-owned packages.
evidence: `constraint_engine.rs` is consumed primarily by OData boundary code (`odata/write.rs`, `odata/bindings.rs`, `odata/common.rs`), but its placement suggests generic server-wide ownership.
`authz_helpers.rs` centralizes observe/API denial handling and header-derived context, but remains a generic root helper rather than an explicit authz module boundary.
This organization makes top-level ownership hard to explain quickly: policy enforcement, request parsing, and routing seams are spread as peer root files.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Create target modules `crates/temper-server/src/authz/mod.rs` (move `security_context_from_headers`, `require_observe_auth`, `record_authz_denial`) and `crates/temper-server/src/odata/constraints.rs` (move `ConstraintViolation*` and relation/invariant check functions). 2) Move call sites in order: OData files first (`odata/common.rs`, `odata/bindings.rs`, `odata/write.rs`), then observe/API auth imports. 3) Keep temporary `pub use` compatibility shims in old files, then remove after import convergence and update `lib.rs`. Import-update/validation commands: `rg -n "crate::(authz_helpers|constraint_engine)::" crates/temper-server/src`, `cargo check -p temper-server --features observe`, `cargo test -p temper-server --features observe`.

findings.summary: Evolution read APIs are Turso-bound while startup provisions Postgres evolution storage
evidence: Observe handlers query only `state.persistent_store()` with comments calling Turso the single source (`records_list.rs`:27,95) and return empty data when no persistent store is present (`:77-85`, `:131-135`).
Postgres startup path migrates `evolution_records` via `PostgresRecordStore` (`storage.rs`:49-53), but that backend is not consumed by these read APIs.
README advertises Postgres/Turso persistence parity and implemented evolution pipeline (`README.md`:41,216,219), so backend behavior is contractually ambiguous.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a backend-neutral evolution read interface in `temper-server` (e.g., `EvolutionReadStore` with `list_evolution_records` and `list_ranked_insights`) and provide adapters for Turso and Postgres record stores. Wire `ServerState` to this trait instead of Turso-specific accessors, then add backend-matrix tests (`cargo test -p temper-server evolution`, with both Postgres and Turso fixtures) to enforce compatibility policy.

findings.summary: Verification guarantees in docs are stronger than persisted restore semantics
evidence: Docs present verification as strict and exhaustive before deployment (`README.md`:111-120).

`row_to_registry_status` reconstructs completed verification from status/booleans when detailed results are absent (`storage.rs`:176-207), fabricating generic level summaries (`:187-191`) rather than requiring original evidence.
This makes the runtime contract after restart materially weaker than the documented governance contract.
findings.suggestion: Define a single verification contract for restore paths: either require persisted `verification_result` as authoritative (fail closed when missing), or explicitly downgrade restored entries to a distinct non-verified status. Update README wording and API/status naming to reflect this lifecycle, and add regression tests for restore-with-missing-result behavior.

findings.summary: Evolution/insight ownership is split across root and observe packages
evidence: `insight_generator.rs` contains core evolution-domain generation logic, while evolution read APIs live in `observe/evolution/records_list.rs`, fragmenting one domain across package boundaries.

`directory_profiles` already show a dedicated evolution package (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/`, 4 files) and a large observe surface (`crates/temper-server/src/observe/`, 10 files), yet key insight logic remains outside it.
README frames evolution as a coherent engine (`README.md`:154,219), but code placement diffuses ownership.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Create `crates/temper-server/src/observe/evolution/pipeline/` and move `insight_generator.rs` to `observe/evolution/pipeline/insight_pipeline.rs`; 2) Extract JSON projection/counting helpers from `records_list.rs` into `observe/evolution/pipeline/response_projection.rs`; 3) Update module exports in `observe/evolution.rs` and call sites; 4) Run import updates with `rg -n "insight_generator|generate_insights|generate_unmet_intents|generate_feature_requests" crates/temper-server/src` and fix paths; 5) Validate with `cargo check -p temper-server` and `cargo test -p temper-server observe::evolution`.

findings.summary: `temper serve` startup path mixes unrelated responsibilities in a single control flow, making changes high-risk.
evidence: `crates/temper-cli/src/serve/mod.rs:60` `run` spans backend selection (`:73-127`), registry recovery/loading (`:129-151`), persisted policy/wasm hydration (`:241-286`), platform bootstrapping (`:301-310`), server startup (`:312-337`), and background job orchestration (`:329-333`).
`spawn_background_verification` in the same file (`:525+`) also mixes event emission, status persistence, registry mutation, CPU-bound verification execution, and console reporting in one function.
`loader.rs` and `storage.rs` already hold extractable concerns, but orchestration still inlines most cross-cutting startup logic.
findings.suggestion: Split startup into explicit phases (storage init, registry hydrate, policy/wasm recover, runtime bootstrap, process wiring) behind a `ServeBootstrapPlan`/`ServeRuntime` boundary; keep `run` as a short phase coordinator with typed phase outputs.

findings.summary: OData read/write handlers repeatedly reimplement the same request workflow, increasing drift risk.
evidence: Write handlers repeat parse/resolve/check scaffolding across methods: `handle_odata_post` (`write.rs:147`), `handle_odata_patch` (`:291`), `handle_odata_put` (`:403`), `handle_odata_delete` (`:494`).

Read handler branches in `handle_odata_get_for_tenant` (`read.rs:281+`) duplicate entity set resolution, missing-entity behavior, and response shaping for `Entity`, `NavigationProperty`, `NavigationEntity`, and `BoundFunction` paths.
Both modules duplicate `EntitySetNotFound`/`ResourceNotFound` shaping and entity existence/lookup checks with slight behavioral differences.
findings.suggestion: Introduce shared OData workflow primitives (e.g., `ResolvedEntityPath`, `load_entity_or_404`, `run_write_prechecks`, `odata_not_found`) and method-specific strategy functions so each endpoint branch focuses only on operation-specific behavior.

findings.summary: Dispatch API remains in a half-migrated state with overlapping entrypoints and split responsibility boundaries.
evidence: `actions.rs` keeps `dispatch`, deprecated `dispatch_action`, `dispatch_tenant_action`, `dispatch_tenant_action_ext`, and `dispatch_tenant_action_core` simultaneously (`:25-153`), so intent is spread across wrapper and core layers.
`dispatch` itself performs extra reaction dispatch orchestration (`:41-63`) while core logic in `dispatch_tenant_action_core` handles actor execution and post-effects, making ownership non-obvious.
Cross-entity side effects in `cross_entity.rs:154+` still call wrapper APIs (`dispatch_tenant_action`) instead of a single canonical internal command surface.
findings.suggestion: Complete the migration to one public dispatch contract plus one internal primitive, move reaction/cross-entity choreography into dedicated effect pipeline steps, and remove legacy wrapper entrypoints after caller migration.

findings.summary: WASM dispatch pipeline conflates execution, policy enforcement, observability, and governance side effects in one path.
evidence: `dispatch_wasm_integrations_internal` (`wasm.rs:24+`) performs integration discovery, module lookup, authz wiring, secret resolution, engine invocation, failure branching, telemetry emission, and callback dispatch in one loop.

`handle_wasm_failure` (`:300+`) adds persistence and metrics concerns, while `record_wasm_authz_denial` (`:420+`) additionally pushes pending decisions, persists asynchronously, creates governance entities, and writes trajectory entries.
Top-level dispatch module (`dispatch/mod.rs`) exposes this as part of core dispatch behavior, further coupling entity-action flow with governance/audit mechanics.
findings.suggestion: Split into composable stages (`resolve_integration`, `invoke_module`, `classify_failure`, `emit_observability`, `emit_governance_effects`) with a typed outcome object; keep governance decision generation as a separate post-invocation handler.

findings.summary: Decision and policy APIs duplicate multi-step authorization+policy-update orchestration instead of sharing a domain service.
evidence: `handle_approve_decision` (`decisions.rs:62+`) performs authz, decision retrieval/validation, policy text generation, engine reload, policy persistence, decision persistence, and evolution record write in one handler.
`handle_put_policies` (`policies.rs:33+`) and `handle_add_policy_rule` (`:135+`) repeat authz denial shaping, JSON validation, combined-policy recomputation, and `reload_policies` orchestration.
Both endpoints reconstruct combined policy text independently (`decisions.rs:131-156`, `policies.rs:95-107`, `:197-219`), creating repeated algorithm paths with different edge-case handling.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared `PolicyMutationService` (authorize → prepare mutation → validate/reload → persist) used by decisions and policies handlers, and centralize denial response construction in one helper to enforce a single mutation workflow.

findings.summary: Request headers can self-assign `System` principal and bypass all authorization checks
evidence: `SecurityContext::from_headers` maps `x-temper-principal-kind=system` to `PrincipalKind::System` (`context.rs:65-74`).
`authorize_or_bypass` returns `Allow` for any system principal without policy evaluation (`engine.rs:217-229`).
Server request path builds security context directly from inbound `x-temper-*` headers (`authz_helpers.rs:17-40`) with no in-process trust-boundary guard.
findings.suggestion: Remove acceptance of `system` from external header parsing and reserve system principals for trusted construction (`SecurityContext::system()` on internal call paths only). Add a server-side middleware that strips or rejects privileged `x-temper-*` identity headers from untrusted requests, and add tests proving external requests cannot obtain system/admin bypass by header spoofing.

findings.summary: Missing tenant header silently falls back to arbitrary/default tenant instead of explicit rejection
evidence: `extract_tenant` chooses first registered tenant when `x-tenant-id` is missing, then falls back to `TenantId::default()` (`odata/common.rs:13-37`).
`get_entity_history` uses this derived tenant for actor lookup and event-store reads (`observe/entities.rs:64-106`).
This makes authorization outcomes depend on registry/default ordering rather than explicit tenant identity.
findings.suggestion: Require an explicit tenant for multi-tenant endpoints: return `400`/`401` when `x-tenant-id` is missing or unknown, and only permit fallback in an explicitly configured single-tenant compatibility mode. Centralize this as a strict tenant resolver shared by OData and observe handlers.

findings.summary: Observe handlers authorize once but return cross-tenant data without tenant-bound filtering
evidence: `require_observe_auth` checks action/resource with empty attrs (`authz_helpers.rs:60-65`), so tenant/resource instance is not part of the decision input.
`list_entities` iterates all actor registry keys and returns all entities regardless of tenant (`observe/entities.rs:30-51`).
`list_specs` iterates `registry.tenant_ids()` and returns every tenant’s specs (`observe/specs.rs:30-67`).
SSE events stream all tenant changes to any caller that passes one global observe permission (`events.rs:47-58`).
findings.suggestion: Introduce tenant- and resource-scoped observe authorization: extract tenant context first, include it in Cedar context/resource attrs, and filter query/stream outputs to authorized tenants only. For cross-tenant admin views, use explicit elevated actions and separate handler paths.

findings.summary: Authorization audit events always attribute tenant as `default`
evidence: Both `authorize` and `authorize_with_context` call `wide_event::from_authz_decision(..., "default")` (`entity_ops.rs:230-237` and `277-284`) instead of using the actual request tenant.
README promises complete governance/auditability for actions (`README.md:25-39`), but hardcoded tenant attribution breaks consistency in multi-tenant traces.
findings.suggestion: Thread resolved tenant through both authorization paths and emit it in wide-event authz telemetry. Add regression tests that verify non-default tenants produce tenant-correct authz decision events.
